"I bought a brick," he replied, shaking his head in dismay. "I can't believe how foolish I've been. Drat!"

"There was this guy on the sidewalk," he explained, "offering three brand-new Panasonic video camera boxes for a steal. He said he needed to get rid of them fast." 

"And then what happened?" 

"He offered to sell me one for just twenty dollars," he continued, "a top-of-the-line four-hundred-dollar camera for a mere twenty bucks!" 

"So, it was a brick?" she questioned. 

"Exactly!" he exclaimed. "I can't believe I fell for it. But he was so convincing. He kept the boxes sealed, claiming the plastic wrap proved they were straight from the store. He said tampering with the 'newness' was a risk." 

"He sealed the boxes in plastic?" she asked in disbelief. 

"Yes," he confirmed. "Impeccably sealed, just like new products. He even left the barcode and price sticker intact. The packaging looked absolutely pristine." 



"But why would someone sell expensive video cameras for twenty dollars?" she wondered aloud.

Han asked John, "What are your plans for the weekend?"
John replied, "I plan to explore Harvard University. I'm hoping to apply there after high school, so I want to learn more about it. What about you?"
Han said, "I meet with my Christian brothers on Saturdays."
"Wow, John, I didn't know you were religious. You seem more like a science geek, like Leonard from The Big Bang Theory," Han joked.
John chuckled, "Come on, Han, don't exaggerate. Many Americans, including scientists, believe in religion."
Han expressed his surprise, "But I thought science and religion are incompatible."
"They can be compatible," John explained. "The U.S. is both a highly developed and very religious country."
Han questioned, "I've heard that before, but I don't understand. How can people believe in God if they know God doesn't exist?"
John replied, "That's a complex topic. You'll understand better when you learn more about U.S. history. Religion has always been a part of American life. Even Harvard University, a very academic institution, was influenced by religion."
Han was intrigued, "Really?"
John affirmed, "Absolutely! It was founded by a group of Puritan immigrants from England."
Han inquired, "Why Puritans? Where were the non-religious educators?"
John explained, "Most of the early English immigrants to the U.S. were Puritans."
Han pressed further, "Why were most of them religious?"
John elaborated, "In the 16th and 17th centuries, there were significant religious reforms in England. Many religious separatists faced persecution and fled to other countries."
Han understood, "So, there wasn't religious freedom in England?"
